title: Honeycomb (cinder toffee)
subtitle: Aerated boiled sugar with bicarbonate
excerpt: British cinder toffee: caster sugar and golden syrup boiled to hard crack, lifted with bicarbonate of soda for the characteristic airy honeycomb structure, poured into a tin to set hard. One slab, broken into shards for a sweet jar.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: documents 'sponge sugar' in the confectionery chapter with the bicarbonate aeration method. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): documents the regional names (honeycomb, cinder toffee, hokey pokey). The Crunchie bar (Cadbury, 1929) standardised the modern form.

recipe.freezeNotes: Honeycomb absorbs moisture and goes sticky; keep in a dry airtight tin.
recipe.batchNotes: Do not double — the aeration foams up enormously and a doubled batch overflows a standard pan.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make ahead and store in an airtight tin for up to 1 week; it loses crispness in humid weather.

recipeTools.notes: Tall and heavy — the syrup foams up to 4× volume when the bicarb hits.
Clip to the side, tip not touching the base.
20 cm square, lined with baking paper.

glossaryTerms.definition: The sugar-cooking stage at 150°C where the syrup, dropped into ice water, hardens into brittle threads that snap cleanly. Above hard crack the sugar begins to caramelise (165°C+ for light caramel; 175°C+ for dark amber). Honeycomb is pulled at hard crack so it sets brittle without darkening into bitter caramel.

content.text: Honeycomb is one of the easier sugar confections: caster sugar and golden syrup are boiled together to hard-crack (150°C), then bicarbonate of soda is whisked in. The bicarb releases carbon dioxide on contact with the hot acidic syrup; the syrup foams up to four times its volume and sets full of fine bubbles. The result is a brittle, airy slab that breaks into shards.

Use a tall, heavy pan — the foaming-up is dramatic and a small pan overflows. A sugar thermometer is essential; the 5°C between soft-crack (135°C) and hard-crack (150°C) is the difference between chewy and brittle.

attrs.title: Hot syrup is dangerous
attrs.body: The syrup reaches 150°C — well above boiling water. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

content.text: Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Combine the {{caster-sugar}}, {{golden-syrup}} and {{water}} in a tall, heavy saucepan. Set over medium heat and stir gently until the sugar dissolves. Stop stirring. Clip the sugar thermometer to the side. Bring to a boil and cook until the temperature reads 150°C — this takes 5–7 minutes. The colour will go from clear to pale gold to deep amber. The colour cue: deep gold, not dark mahogany; pull it before it darkens further.
Whisk in the bicarb, pour, do not stir

Take the pan off the heat. Add the sifted {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} all at once and whisk for 5 seconds; the syrup will foam up dramatically. As soon as the bicarb is incorporated, pour the foaming mixture into the lined tin — do not scrape, do not spread, do not stir. Let the bubbles set as they are. Leave to set hard at room temperature for 30 minutes.

Lift the slab out of the tin using the paper. Break into rough shards by hitting with the back of a heavy spoon. Store in an airtight tin in a dry cupboard; honeycomb absorbs moisture and goes sticky if left exposed.
